title: Making a mitred picture frame
subtitle: Cut and assemble a timber picture frame with mitred corner joints
excerpt: How to measure, mark, cut, glue, and cramp a mitred picture frame from moulding timber, using a mitre saw or mitre box to cut accurate 45-degree corners and finishing the assembled frame ready for priming or staining.
sourceNotes: The Complete Manual of Woodworking, Albert Jackson and David Day. Collins Complete DIY Manual. Ronseal technical data sheets.

glossaryTerms.definition: A step or groove cut into the back inner edge of a picture frame moulding that holds the glass, mount, artwork, and backing board. Ready-made picture frame moulding comes with the rebate already cut; the rebate depth must be at least as deep as the combined thickness of glass, mount, and board.

A cut made at 45° across the width of a piece of timber so that when two pieces are joined at their cut faces, they form a right-angle corner. The mitre angle divides the 90° corner equally between both pieces. A correctly cut mitre shows no gap at the face of the corner.

A professional frame-joining tool that drives V-shaped metal wedges up into the face of a mitred corner from below, pulling the joint tight. The standard tool in frame shops; at home, corner cramps and pins achieve the same result but more slowly.

content.text: The quality of a mitred frame depends almost entirely on the accuracy of the
. A gap at one corner of 1 mm over a 400 mm frame face is visible from 3 metres. The measurement convention is to cut the mitres so that the inside dimension of the frame: the
opening: matches the size of the artwork, not the outside of the frame.
Step 1, measure and mark the first piece

Mark the inside length of the first long side on the face of the moulding: the rebate dimension, not the outside. Mark the position of the two 45° mitre cuts so that the short (inside) points of the mitres define the inside length. Cut the first piece and use it as a direct template to mark the opposite long side, placing the cut piece against the moulding stock and transferring the marks. This method avoids the accumulation of measurement error.

Cut all four pieces with the mitre saw set to exactly 45°. For the two long sides, the mitres at each end slope in opposite directions. For the two short sides, the same applies. Dry-fit all four corners on a flat surface: each joint should close without a gap when held by hand. A consistent gap at all four corners indicates the saw is cutting at slightly more or less than 45°; adjust the saw angle by half the error.
Step 3, glue and cramp the frame

Apply a thin coat of PVA wood glue to both faces of each mitre. Assemble the frame flat on the bench and pull the corners tight with corner cramps or wrap a web cramp around all four corners. Check the frame is square by measuring the diagonals: they must be equal. A frame that is assembled square but then clamped out of square by uneven cramp pressure will dry crooked. Leave under cramp for at least 1 hour before removing.

Once the glue has set, drive a 40 mm oval nail or panel pin through the face of each corner, parallel to and 10 mm from each face of the joint. This locks each corner against the glue joint failing under the lateral load of a hanging picture. Set the pin heads below the surface with a nail punch. Fill the pin holes with a wood filler tinted to match the moulding, sand flush when dry.

replaces the separate gluing and pinning steps. It is a professional tool and costs significantly more than a hammer and pins, but is much faster for batch work. For a box frame (for three-dimensional objects), use a deeper moulding with a larger rebate and add a spacer section behind the main frame to create depth.

attrs.symptom: Gaps at all four corners on the face side
attrs.cause: Mitre saw angle is not exactly 45° — cuts are too steep or too shallow.
attrs.fix: Adjust the saw by half the apparent error and cut test pieces from scrap until four pieces assemble without gaps. Do not attempt to sand or plane the error out of already-cut pieces.

attrs.symptom: Frame is square when measured but corners open after uncramping
attrs.cause: Glue not fully cured when cramps were removed; cramp pressure applied unevenly.
attrs.fix: Reapply glue, re-cramp, and leave for at least 2 hours. For PVA in cold conditions (below 10°C), extend curing time to 4 hours.

attrs.symptom: Artwork fits the rebate opening but the glass does not
attrs.cause: Glass cut to the artwork size rather than 1 mm smaller than the rebate opening on each dimension.
attrs.fix: Have the glass recut 1 mm smaller on each dimension. Glass should drop freely into the rebate with a small clearance.
